_meta.note: v4.0 rebuilt from ground up using verified CoP clause extraction. Each control = one CoP sub-subsection. Each requirement = one verbatim CoP clause with original clause number. Tier 1 = SHALL (compliance-rated P/SP/TP/TB). Tier 2 = SHOULD/MAY (improvement opportunity). Directive-only requirements (42) are NOT in this file — they belong in separate directive compliance sheets.

requirements.text: Clear roles and responsibilities for cyber security at all levels within the organisation shall be established, ensuring that there is accountability for securing the NCII computer and computer systems.

controls.name: Establishment of the Cyber Security Policy

requirements.text: The NCII entity shall establish a cyber security policy that outlines the organisation's overall approach to cyber security, and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, in accordance with Act 854 and other written laws.
This policy should demonstrate the NCII entity's commitment to meeting cyber security requirements and aligning with the organisation's strategic direction.

The policy shall align with recognised industry standards, best practices and frameworks such as follows, to ensure effectiveness of the policy:
- ISO/IEC 27001:2022 -- Information Security, cybersecurity and privacy protection -- Information security management systems -- Requirements
- ISO/IEC 27002:2022 -- Information Security, cybersecurity and privacy protection -- Information Security Controls
- The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Cybersecurity Framework
- Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10
- National and sector-specific cyber security regulations

controls.name: Application of the Cyber Security Policy

requirements.text: The policy shall be applied across the NCII entity's organisation, including all the NCII owned or operated by the NCII entity as well as all personnel who act on or have access to an NCII.

controls.name: Review of the Cyber Security Policy

requirements.text: The policy shall be reviewed by the relevant team such as the top management and relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, and at least annually and / or whenever significant changes occur such as adoption of new technologies, change made to the design, configuration, security or operation, possession or ceased of the NCII, regulatory requirements or organisational structure impacting cyber security governance (NCII entity no longer owns or operates any NCII).

A structured policy review process may include the following:
- Designation of responsible personnel for conducting the review.
- Documenting revisions, justifications, and approvals.
- Version control and change management procedures.

The policy shall be proactively communicated to all relevant stakeholders, through methods of communication such as awareness programs and training sessions, intranet knowledge bases and other methods of communication deemed appropriate.
A communication plan may be established to ensure the Cyber Security Policy is disseminated, understood, and enforced across all levels of the organisation.

The NCII entity shall develop cyber security objectives that are align with its cyber security policy, standards such as Information Security Management System (ISMS), and compliance to the laws and regulations, towards enabling cyber resilience in digitalization.
The objectives must be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound.
The objectives shall be effectively communicated to relevant stakeholders, to ensure awareness, responsibility and accountability.
The NCII entity must establish a structured communication plan that ensures all relevant stakeholders understand their roles and responsibilities in achieving cyber security objectives.

The objectives shall be continuously monitored, tracked and reported annually to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, through a monitoring and reporting framework.
The NCII entity should have a monitoring mechanism to validate compliance with the objectives.

A cyber security report shall be submitted to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, outlining progress towards achieving cyber security objectives, identified areas of concern and resource needs.

The NCII entity shall establish well-defined cyber security roles and responsibilities that includes:
- Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / ICT Security Officer (ICTSO) or equivalent
- Cyber security team members (analysts, engineers, incident responders)
- Information Technology (IT) staff (for supporting cyber security functions)
Each role shall have a documented description, including job scopes, level of authority and reporting lines.

The defined cyber security roles and responsibilities shall be formally documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders, that can be disseminated through the following:
- Employee handbook
- Training sessions
- Security briefings
Manpower and financial resources shall be assigned according to the defined cyber security roles and responsibilities.
The NCII entity shall ensure that sufficient qualified personnel are available to effectively implement, manage and maintain cyber security controls.

A dedicated cyber security budget shall be allocated to support the implementation of the cyber security strategy that includes the following:
- Cyber security tools (firewalls, antivirus, monitoring systems)
- Training and capacity building
- Cyber security risks, audits and compliance assessments

The allocation of cyber security resources shall be reviewed annually and / or updated as necessary, based on factors such as needs, auditor recommendations, and any relevant considerations by the NCII entity, including the likelihood or impact of risks.

Input for the review shall be obtained from the following stakeholders:
- Cyber security team / IT department
- Human Resources department
- Finance department

The NCII entity shall establish contacts with relevant authorities and special interest groups, including:
- National Cyber Coordination and Command Centre (NC4), National Cyber Security Agency (NACSA)
- Royal Malaysia Police (RMP)
The NCII entity shall maintain an up-to-date list, including direct phone numbers, email addresses, and jurisdiction details of the relevant authorities and special interest groups.

The contact information for relevant authorities and special interest groups shall be shared with stakeholders, ensuring all relevant NCII entity employees have access to it for reporting cyber security incidents or seeking cyber security guidance.

Communication channels shall be established through medium communication such as information security bulletin / handbook, the NCII entity's intranet portal, Crisis Communication Plan and other medium that is deemed appropriate.
The list of cyber security authorities and special interest groups shall be reviewed annually and / or in response to significant changes, such as updates to regulatory bodies or industry standards.
Any updates to the list must be promptly communicated to all employees to ensure awareness and compliance.

The NCII entity shall develop and establish a cyber security awareness and training plan for specific target audience, covering the following topics:
- Social engineering awareness
- Cyber hygiene / best practices of the following:
  - authentication procedure/password management
  - clear screen / clean desk
- Cyber security incident reporting procedures
- Safe handling of sensitive data, pertaining to the NCII entity's Data Privacy and Protection Policy
The content of the security awareness training program must be reviewed and updated at least annually, or whenever significant changes to the organisation occur.
The training plan must be documented and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management.

Cyber security awareness or training programs shall be conducted across the NCII entity at a frequency determined by the organisation. All new employees must complete cyber security awareness training before being granted access to the NCII entity's assets.

Awareness and training methods may include:
- Workshop and seminars
- Quizzes
- Infographic posters
- Simulated phishing attacks

Upon each program completion, its effectiveness shall be evaluated using methods such as:
- Post-program assessments (quizzes or tests)
- Participant feedback surveys
- Cyber security incident analysis during simulations
A pre-program assessment may be conducted to establish a baseline, which will be compared with post-program assessment results.

controls.name: Establishment of IT Acceptable Use Policy

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall develop and establish a comprehensive IT Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) that outlines the following:
- Acceptable use of enterprise assets
- Prohibited use of enterprise assets
- Personal use of enterprise assets
- Privacy expectations while working with enterprise assets
- Reporting violations of the IT AUP
- Remote work policies
- Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) policy (either enabling or restricting the BYOD)
The IT AUP shall align with the sector's industry best practices, standards and regulatory requirements.

controls.name: Application of IT Acceptable Use Policy

requirements.text:
The IT AUP shall be enforced across the NCII entity through the following recommended means:
- Letter of Undertaking
- Technical configuration
The IT support team shall train employees on IT AUP compliance through platforms such as onboarding training and information bulletins.

controls.name: Review of IT Acceptable Use Policy

requirements.text:
The IT AUP shall be reviewed and updated when necessary, such as in the following circumstances:
- Emergence of new cyber security threats
- Changes in technology or regulatory requirements
- Identification of gaps from cyber security incidents

The NCII entity shall establish an audit plan on cyber security that defines the following:
- Audit scope
- Audit objectives
- Audit methodology
The audit plan on cyber security shall be aligned with legal, regulatory, and organisational requirements.
The audit plan on cyber security shall include the frequency of audit, audit criteria, and roles and responsibilities of the auditors.

The audit plan on cyber security shall be reviewed and approved by relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, ensuring the following:
- Alignment with the NCII entity's objectives
- Sufficient allocation of resources (manpower and budgets)
- Compliance to the Act 854
Any amendments to the audit plan on cyber security must be documented and re-approved before the implementation.

controls.name: Conducting the Audit on Cyber Security

requirements.text: Audit on cyber security shall be conducted at least once in every two years commencing from the date of designation as NCII entity or at such higher frequency as may be directed by the Chief Executive of NACSA in any particular case as stated in the Cyber Security (Period for Cyber Security Risk Assessment and Audit) Regulations 2024 [P.U. (A) 219/2024].

The auditor shall be approved by Chief Executive of NACSA to assess the compliance with Act 854 and its regulations, directives of the Chief Executive of NACSA, code of practice, and other cyber security practices adopted by the NCII entity.
The audit process shall include document reviews, interviews, system testing, and technical assessments of cyber security controls.

When an NCII entity receives a written notice from the Chief Executive directing the NCII entity to conduct an audit in relation to any material changes made to the design, configuration, security, or operation of an NCII, the NCII entity shall carry out such an audit in respect of the NCII, regardless of whether an audit on cyber security has been conducted or carried out under subsection 22(1) of Act 854 in relation to that NCII.
Audit findings shall be documented in a structured report, highlighting compliance status, gaps, and areas for improvement.
The audit report shall be presented and reported to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management and stakeholders.
The audit report shall be submitted to the Chief Executive of NACSA within thirty (30) days after the completion of the cyber security audit.
Any non-compliance identified during the audit shall be addressed with corrective and preventive actions.
A remediation plan should be created, detailing the necessary steps, assigned personnel, and deadlines for resolution.

Progress on remediation efforts shall be monitored, with regular updates provided to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management and relevant stakeholders.
Follow-up audits may be conducted to ensure the effectiveness of corrective measures.

controls.name: Establishment of Supply Chain Management Policy

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall define a Supply Chain Management (SCM) Policy to ensure the following:
- Proactively identify, assess and mitigate cyber security risks associated with supplier relationships that covers internal and external services, processes, network connection, scripts, equipment, business partners.
- Third-party suppliers adhere to a defined cyber security baseline to safeguard the NCII entity's assets and operations.
- Secure handling, storage and transmission of shared confidential data throughout the supply chain.

The SCM Policy shall include the following:
- Suppliers must demonstrate compliance with recognised security standards such as ISO/IEC 27001 certification or equivalent frameworks.
- The NCII entity engaging external service providers for cyber security services relating to managed security operation centre monitoring and penetration testing shall appoint a cyber security service provider holding a valid license issued under NACSA, in accordance with subregulation 2(1) of the Cyber Security (Licensing of Cyber Security Service Provider) Regulations 2024 [P.U. (A) 221/2024]. These requirements do not apply if the cyber security service provider falls under subregulation 2(2) of P.U. (A) 221/2024, as follows--
  - The cyber security service is provided by a Government Entity;
  - The cyber security service is provided by a person, other than a company, to its related company; or
  - the computer or computer system in respect of which the cyber security service is provided is located outside Malaysia.
- Supplier agreements shall incorporate cyber security requirements and policies to ensure external parties comply with the NCII entity's security standards. These agreements must include specific contractual terms outlining data protection measures, security assessments and compliance with applicable laws and regulations.
- Establish processes for continuous monitoring and evaluation of supplier adherence to the SCM Policy.

controls.name: Identification and Documentation of External Service Providers

requirements.text:
All external service providers handling IT infrastructure, software or sensitive data shall be identified and documented in an external service provider register that documents the following data:
- Provider Information: Name, contact details, and role of the service provider.
- Scope of Services: Description of the services provided and their relevance to the entity's operations.
- Data Handling: Specifics of any sensitive or confidential data accessed, processed, or stored by the provider.
- Risk Level: Assessment of the cyber security and operational risks associated with the provider.
- Compliance Status: Documentation of the provider's adherence to relevant security standards (ISO/IEC 27001) and contractual obligations.
- Contract Details: Reference to any agreements or contracts in place, including clauses related to data protection, security requirements, and breach notification.

controls.name: Review of Supply Chain Management Policy

requirements.text: The SCM Policy and external service provider register shall be reviewed annually to ensure ongoing compliance with evolving security standards.
The review process shall include, but is not limited to risk reassessment, compliance verification and vendor performance evaluation.

The NCII entity shall establish an IT assets inventory, listing all the computer or computer system as the following:
- End-user devices (desktops, workstations, laptops, smartphones)
- Network devices (wireless access points, switches, firewalls, routers)
- Internet of Things (IoT) devices (printers, smart television)
- Servers (web servers, email servers, application servers)
- Software (operating systems, free software, freeware, open source, in-house, scripts).
The inventory shall be centrally managed and updated in an asset management system.

Each IT assets shall be recorded with:
- Unique identification number
- Date of acquisition
- Purchase price / Value of assets
- Type and category
- Manufacturer
- Model / Serial number
- Location and assigned department
- Asset owner
- Asset lifecycle status / Warranty expiration date

In instances where the NCII entity acquires control over a computer or computer system that, in its assessment, qualifies as part of the NCII, the NCII entity shall report this information to the NCII sector lead.

If there are any material changes to the NCII following the initial submission of information, where such a change impacts or has the potential to impact the cyber security of the NCII or the NCII entity's ability to respond to cyber security threats or incidents, the NCII entity shall formally notify the NCII sector lead within thirty (30) days from the date of the change's completion.

IT assets inventory shall be maintained and continuously updated to reflect:
- Newly acquired assets
- Decommissioned or retired assets
- Software license validity
- Ownership or department changes
A maintenance plan shall be established, incorporating quarterly preventive maintenance and corrective maintenance, along with the associated costs.
All IT assets shall be classified based on their value, sensitivity level, and the risks they pose to the NCII entity.
Priority shall be given to securing funding and resources to adequately protect these assets, ensuring that risks are effectively mitigated in alignment with their classification.

The sensitivity level of an IT assets shall be classified as follows, and labelled accordingly:
- Public
- Restricted
- Confidential
- Secret
- Top Secret

Each asset shall have an assigned owner responsible for:
- Security controls and compliance.
- Lifecycle management (updates, renewals, decommissioning).
- Cyber security incident reporting related to the asset.
The IT assets inventory shall be reviewed annually and / or whenever changes occur, such as the acquisition of a new systems or the decommissioning of old assets.

The review shall validate:
- Asset ownership
- Current status (in use, not in use, damaged, under maintenance, loaned, lost)
- Asset disposal
- Software license validity

The NCII entity shall establish a Capacity Management Plan that ensures the following:
- IT resources are adequate to meet current and future demands.
- Systems are resilient under peak loads and unexpected surges.
- Business continuity planning includes capacity considerations.

IT support team shall continuously track and monitor the resource utilisation of the following:
- CPU and memory usage.
- Network bandwidth consumption.
- Disk space availability.
- Application performance metrics.

Resource utilisation shall be reviewed annually, and forecasts shall be developed for:
- Future system growth (new users, additional workloads).
- Infrastructure upgrades (new servers, storage expansions).
Resource forecasting shall use historical data and predictive analytics to estimate future IT resource needs.

Performance testing shall be conducted prior to system go-live or whenever required, including after major system changes or based on cyber security risk assessment results to validate and confirm the resilience of the system's performance.

Performance tests shall simulate:
- High traffic surges (peak demand periods).
- Data processing loads (large database queries).
- Network congestion scenarios.

controls.name: Establishment of Cyber Security Risk Assessment Process

requirements.text: The NCII entity shall establish a cyber security risk assessment process to support informed decision-making and the management of cyber security risks.
The process shall be formally documented and integrated into the NCII entity's governance framework.
The cyber security risk assessment process shall align with industry best practices (ISO 27001, NIST 800-53, Information Security Risk Management Guideline (PRKM)).
The NCII entity shall adopt recognised methodologies for risk evaluation and mitigation.

Cyber security risk assessments shall be conducted annually commencing from the date of designation as NCII entity as stated in the P.U. (A) 219/2024 and whenever significant changes occur (material change made to the design, configuration, security or operation of the entities' NCII, changes of technology and any incident that happened in the past).
The scope of the cyber security risk assessment conducted shall include an assessment of risks to the computer or computer systems designated as NCII owned or operated by the NCII entity.

The cyber security risk assessment shall include the following key steps:
- Risk Identification: Proactively identify potential cyber security risks, including assets inventory, cyber security threats, vulnerabilities and potential strategic scenarios on the NCII entity's operations, assets, and stakeholders.
- Risk Analysis: Assess the level of risk based on the likelihood and impact of identified risks, taking into account existing controls and their effectiveness.
- Risk Evaluation: Evaluate and prioritise risks by comparing the assessed level of risk against the predefined risk criteria established by the NCII entity.

The cyber security risk assessment results shall be documented and reported to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this, as authorised by the management, and stakeholders including the Chief Executive of NACSA, within the period of thirty (30) days after the completion of the cyber security risk assessment.
The report shall be submitted to the Chief Executive of NACSA via e-mail of auditpematuhan@nacsa.gov.my and the NCII entity's sector lead.
In cases where the submitted report requires rectification, the Chief Executive of NACSA may determine an alternative timeframe for re-evaluation and re-submission.

Cyber security risk reports shall include the following:
- The methodology used in the cyber security risk assessment
- Description of each identified cyber security risk to the NCII
- The likelihood of occurrence and impact of the identified cyber security risks
- The prioritisation of cyber security risks
- The actions to be taken by the NCII entity to address each of the identified risks

Identified cyber security risks shall be prioritised based on the level of risks and the NCII entity's acceptance criteria. Risks classified as high shall be addressed with immediate treatment to reduce their impact and likelihood.

When evaluating risks against the acceptance criteria, the NCII entity shall consider potential impacts, including reputational damage, legal consequences, financial losses and harm to stakeholders and the organisation as a whole.
The NCII entity shall formulate a Risk Treatment Plan (RTP) that outlines prioritised actions based on the level of risk and the urgency of treatment required.

For each identified cyber security risk within the RTP, detailed implementation information shall be monitored and documented. This information shall include, but is not limited to:
- Risk treatment option: Option for risk treatment may include risk retention, risk modification, risk sharing and risk avoidance.
- Risk treatment controls: Specific measures or actions to mitigate the risk.
- Designation of risk owners: Individuals or teams accountable for managing the risk.
- Designation of persons responsible for implementation: Individuals or teams tasked with executing the treatment activity.
- Residual risk: The level of risk remaining after the implementation of treatment controls.
The RTP shall be reviewed and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee as authorised by management, prior to implementation.
To grant approval, the risk owners shall evaluate the proposed controls outlined in the RTP to assess their effectiveness in mitigating the likelihood and/ or the impact of the identified risks.

The approved RTP shall be implemented and the implementation effectiveness should be continuously monitored and reviewed during the meetings of relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management.

Progress on the execution of the plan shall be reported periodically, with clear status updates categorised as:
- Not Started: Tasks or controls that have yet to be initiated.
- In Progress: Tasks or controls currently being implemented.
- Completed: Tasks or controls that have been fully executed.

The results of the cyber security risk treatment shall be documented and reported to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, and / or stakeholders.

The report should include, but not limited to, the following:
- The necessary controls for risk treatment; and
- The evidence demonstrates that these controls effectively mitigate risks to align with the NCII entity's risk acceptance criteria (where applicable and feasible).
The NCII entity shall establish a Change Management Procedure to ensure structured and controlled changes to information processing facilities, information systems and other relevant IT components.

The procedure shall outline:
- Change request submission and documentation.
- Assessment of risks and impact.
- Testing and validation steps.
- Implementation and rollback strategies.
The change management procedure shall be enforced across all NCII entity to maintain system stability and security.
All changes shall be tracked in a centralised system for accountability.
Emergency changes shall require expedited approvals and post-implementation reviews.
Any changes to information processing facilities, information systems or other relevant IT components, shall require formal authorisation from system owners prior to implementation.

Authorisation shall be granted based on:
- Risk level.
- Business impact.
- Security implications.
The change management procedure shall be reviewed annually and / or when significant changes occur.

Reviews shall assess:
- Effectiveness of the change control process.
- Compliance with security and operational policies.
- Efficiency of the approval and rollback mechanisms.
The NCII entity shall implement a DLP solution across its organisation, to safeguard sensitive data from unauthorised access, leakage, or theft.

The DLP solution shall include:
- Endpoint protection for monitoring data transfers via devices (USB, external drives).
- Network protection to inspect data transfers via email, file sharing, or cloud uploads.
- Data classification to identify and tag sensitive data (PII, intellectual property).
Regular maintenance shall be applied to the DLP solution.

controls.name: Integration with Security Information and Event Monitoring (SIEM)

requirements.text:
NCII entity shall ensure that DLP logs is integrated seamlessly with the SIEM tool to:
- Provide centralised monitoring and analysis of cyber security incidents.
- Facilitate correlation of events to detect suspicious patterns.
- Enhance cyber security incident response capabilities through automated alerts and escalations.

controls.name: Establishment of Data Privacy and Protection Procedure

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall define a comprehensive procedure for managing data privacy and protection, including data management lifecycle as follows:
- Data acquisition: When acquiring data, the NCII entity must consider storage solutions and security requirements that are commensurate with the type of data collected.
- Data inventory: The IT department must conduct an inventory of data on an annual basis. All sensitive data must be marked accordingly in the inventory. Components of a data inventory may include the data type, data owner, data classification, data location, data format and data retention. Data backup is crucial, and the appropriate data to back up must be identified by utilising the data inventory to support the identification process.
- Data classification: The NCII entity should determine what data is considered sensitive based on legal, regulatory, or business requirements as well as potential risk posed should data be compromised or lost.
- Data protection: IT department must configure access control lists on NCII entity's assets in accordance with user's need to know. Sensitive data should be encrypted when in transit or at rest on servers, applications and databases.
- Data handling: The NCII entity should properly secure the data throughout the whole lifecycle including the processing, storage, and transmission of the data, in accordance with data sensitivity. All data and documents must be preserved for the appropriate amount of time as dictated by regulatory, legal, and business requirements.
- Data disposal: Authorised parties must destroy data that have outlasted their specified retention timeframes. Whenever possible, the enterprise should ensure that contracts with third-party services providers include clauses to destroy all company data at the request of the enterprise. Ensure that the disposal process and method are appropriate for the sensitivity of the data.

controls.name: Alignment with Legal and Regulatory Requirements
Application of Data Privacy and Protection Procedure

requirements.text:
The procedure shall be applied across the NCII entity that covers:
- All operational processes involving personal or sensitive data, from acquisition to disposal.
- Third-party engagements with third parties, including vendors, contractors, and partners, ensuring data sharing and processing agreements comply with the organisation's policy.
- Include data protection clauses in all contracts and conduct regular assessments of third-party compliance.
Compliance with the data privacy and protection procedure shall be regularly monitored, through audits and assessments of the data privacy and protection practices.

Monitoring shall include:
- Data access logs to detect unauthorised activities.
- Assessments of access controls.
- Periodic staff training to reinforce compliance awareness.

controls.name: Review of Data Privacy and Protection Procedure

requirements.text:
The procedure shall be reviewed at least annually and / or whenever necessary, such as:
- Adoption of new technologies or data processes.
- Updates to applicable laws or regulations.
- Changes to the organization's structure, processes, or risk profile.
Record of all reviews, including findings, recommendations, and implemented changes shall be maintained.

The NCII entity shall establish cyber security guidelines for the development of software and system / application that covers, but not limited to the following:
- Secure coding practices that align with recognised security guidelines (OWASP Top 10).
- Access control mechanisms within applications.
- Requirements for regular updates and patches.

The guidelines shall be enforced during the acquisition of software and system / application, either through the software development lifecycle (SDLC) stages or the procurement of third-party software solutions.

Cyber security requirements for each software and system / application shall be identified and specified during development or acquisition process. These requirements shall be reviewed and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee as authorised by management for this purpose.

The NCII entity shall ensure security testing on software and system / application are conducted during development followed by penetration testing prior to deployment in a production environment and public facing.

Pre-deployment security testing shall include:
- Vulnerability scans to detect weaknesses.
- Penetration testing to simulate attack scenarios.
- Validation of compliance with secure coding guidelines.

NCII entity shall establish a configuration procedure that defines the baseline for the technologies used across the following asset categories before they are deployed by the NCII entity:
- Hardware
- Software
- Services
- Networks

In the event of the unavailability of the configuration guidelines for a specific technology, the NCII entity's IT department must research and determine the appropriate security configurations, and develop a configuration template before deploying the product.
Technologies with secure configurations shall be continuously monitored and reviewed to ensure ongoing compliance with approved settings.

The approved secure configuration guidelines for a technology must be promptly updated following any significant updates. The criteria for determining "significant" updates shall be established based on industry standards and defined thresholds.
Any significant updates and installations shall be approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management.
The configuration logs shall be able to record all changes, approvals, cyber security incidents and audit activities.

The logs must be securely maintained to support regulatory compliance and forensic investigations. This can be achieved by retaining the data retention for a defined period (e.g. five (5) years) as determined by the relevant cyber security governance established for this purpose.

The NCII entity shall establish NDAs and develop standardised agreement templates to ensure consistency and compliance with legal requirements. These templates must:
- Define confidential information (customer data, project details).
- Specify the obligations of parties (protection, non-sharing, and non-disclosure of information).
- Outline the permitted use of confidential information (use strictly for intended purposes).
- Include consequences for breaches of confidentiality.

NDAs shall be complied with:
- Legal and regulatory requirements.
- Industry standards and best practices.

NDAs shall be signed by all relevant parties before sharing or granting access to sensitive information. This includes:
- Internal stakeholders (employees, contractors, interns).
- External stakeholders (vendors, suppliers, business partners).

A designated authority or legal representative within the NCII entity shall:
- Ensure that all parties understand their responsibilities and obligations.
- Verify that signed agreements are stored securely in a centralised repository.
- Only authorised personnel should have access to these documents to prevent unauthorized modifications or breaches.

The NCII entity shall monitor compliance with signed NDAs through:
- Regular audits and reviews of information-sharing practices.
- Implementation of access controls to track and log the flow of confidential information.
- Periodic re-confirmation of NDA obligations with long-term collaborators or employees.
Instances of non-compliance shall be reported to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, for further action.
The NCII entity shall establish a comprehensive physical security procedure to safeguard its premises, personnel, and assets.

The procedure shall address:
- Access control mechanisms (ID badges, biometric systems).
- Surveillance and monitoring (CCTV, security personnel).
- Visitor management processes (visitor logs, escort policies).
- Emergency response plans (fire drills, evacuation routes).

The physical security procedure shall adhere to industry standards and best practices, such as:
- ISO 27001 for information security management.
- National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) standards for fire and life safety.
- Local and international legal regulations for physical safety and security.

The physical security procedure shall be implemented across all NCII entity's premises, including:
- Headquarters and branch offices.
- Data centres and server rooms.
- Storage areas for sensitive documents or equipment.
Employees, contractors, and visitors shall be trained and informed about the procedure to ensure compliance.

Compliance with the physical security procedure shall be monitored through:
- Regular inspections and audits of facilities.
- Access control system logs to track entry and exit activities.
- Reports from security personnel or surveillance systems.
Any instances of non-compliance shall be documented and addressed promptly.
The physical security procedure shall be reviewed annually as part of routine organisational assessments, and / or whenever significant changes occur, such as relocation, expansion, or new risks.

Reviews shall involve relevant stakeholders, including:
- Security teams.
- Facilities management.
- Legal and compliance officers.
The NCII entity shall establish security perimeters to prevent unauthorised physical access, damage and interference to the NCII's information.

The security perimeters may include the following:
- Outer perimeters (fences, gates).
- Internal perimeters (restricted access zones, server rooms).
- High-security zones (areas with sensitive or classified information).
Clear signage shall indicate authorised access zones and restricted areas.

Entry points shall be equipped with:
- Locks, turnstiles, or electronic access systems.
- Video surveillance and alarms for unauthorised access attempts.

Continuous monitoring of the NCII entity's premises shall be implemented to prevent unauthorised physical access such as the following:
- CCTV systems shall cover all critical and restricted areas, with footage archived for at least thirty (30) days or as required by policy.
- Security personnel shall conduct regular patrols and maintain logs of their observations.
- Visitor and access logs shall be reviewed periodically to detect irregularities.

The NCII entity shall design and implement measures to protect against physical and environmental threats to reduce the consequences of unwanted events, such as:
- Natural disasters (floods, earthquakes):
  - Use of reinforced structures.
  - Emergency evacuation plans.
- Fire hazards:
  - Fire detection and suppression systems (sprinklers, extinguishers).
  - Regular fire drills and training.
- Theft or vandalism:
  - Secure storage of valuable assets.
  - Alarm systems for unauthorised entry.
A risk assessment shall be conducted regularly to identify potential threats and implement corresponding mitigation measures.

controls.name: Establishment of Network Security Policy and Procedure

requirements.text: The NCII entity shall develop and document a comprehensive network security policy and procedure to protect network infrastructure and maintain secure operations.

The policy and procedure shall address enforcement of access controls based on authentication and least privilege principles, using models like Role-based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-based Access Control (ABAC), or other relevant approaches suited to the NCII entity's needs.
Key stakeholders, including IT teams and security personnel, shall be involved in the drafting process.

The network security policy and procedure shall be reviewed and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee as authorised by management for this purpose, to ensure:
- Organisational alignment and resource allocation.
- Accountability for enforcement at all levels.

The network security policy and procedure shall adhere to industry standards and best practices such as:
- ISO 27001: Information Security Management.
- NIST Cybersecurity Framework.

Compliance with the network security policy and procedure shall be monitored through:
- Regular internal audits and assessments.
- Usage of automated tools to track policy adherence.
- Reporting of violations or deviations from the policy.

controls.name: Review of Network Security Policy and Procedure

requirements.text: The network security policy and procedure shall be reviewed annually as part of the NCII entity's security assessments, and / or when significant changes occur in the network or cyber security threat landscape.

controls.name: Establishment of Physical and Logical Network Diagrams

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall define and maintain physical and logical network diagrams that include:
- Physical diagrams: Representation of hardware, cabling, and device locations.
- Logical diagrams: Network segmentation, IP addressing, and data flows.
These diagrams shall provide a comprehensive understanding of the network architecture.
Network diagrams shall be aligned with industry standards and best practices such as NIST SP 800-115, to ensure clarity, accuracy and effectiveness in troubleshooting and strategic planning.

The diagrams shall include advanced security controls, such as:
- Next-generation firewalls (NGFW): For application-aware traffic filtering.
- Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS): For detecting and mitigating cyber security threats.
- SIEM: For real-time monitoring and analysis.
- Zero Trust Architecture: To ensure granular access controls.

controls.name: Review of Physical and Logical Network Diagrams

requirements.text:
Network diagrams shall undergo review and updates by the relevant cyber security governance committee, as authorised by the management, when changes occur, including:
- The addition of new network components.
- Modifications to security controls.

The NCII entity shall implement firewall rules to control traffic between network segments by:
- Enforcing traffic segmentation between different network zones.
- Blocking unauthorised ports and protocols.
- Regularly updating firewall rules to address emerging cyber security threats.

The NCII entity shall implement network threat detection capability to ensure timely detection and response to suspicious network activities to prevent and mitigate cyber security threats. This includes the use of a Web Application Firewall (WAF) for all external-facing applications to mitigate common attack vectors.
Threat detection capabilities shall be tested and refined regularly to adapt to evolving cyber risks.

The NCII entity shall implement NAC solutions across the organisation to enforce access policies, including:
- Device authentication and authorisation before granting network access.
- Quarantining non-compliant devices.
- Segmentation based on device risk levels.

Firewall rules, network threat detection capability, and NAC solutions shall be reviewed annually to ensure they align with security needs, and / or whenever necessary, such as after major network updates or incidents.

controls.name: Implementation of Malware Detection and Prevention Software

requirements.text: Malware detection and prevention software shall be deployed across all endpoints, servers, and network devices to prevent or control the installation, spread and execution of malicious applications, code or scripts on the NCII entity's assets.

Features of the malware detection and prevention software shall include:
- Real-time scanning for malware and viruses.
- Automatic updates.
- Quarantine or removal of malicious files.
NCII entities user must not disable the malware detection and prevention software on their assets.
The software must be configured to automatically update and IT support must ensure that the malware signatures are kept up-to-date as they become available.

controls.name: Integration with Security Information and Event Monitoring (SIEM)

requirements.text: Logs from malware detection and prevention software shall be integrated with tools, such as SIEM, that enable centralised monitoring and correlation of events to detect sophisticated attacks.
Logs generated by the malware detection and prevention software shall be regularly analysed for patterns or anomalies, and evaluated for emerging malware trends or tactics.
Identified malware must be removed from the NCII entity's assets.

controls.name: Establishment of Identity and Access Management Procedures

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall define and implement an Identity and Access Management (IAM) procedure that includes:
- Types of accounts and credential management that such as:
  - Administrative accounts: Also referred to as superuser, privileged, or root accounts, these accounts possess extensive permissions to control and manage operating systems, applications, and platforms.
  - User accounts: These accounts are normally used by employees and contractors to perform their day-to-day work tasks. While they typically have limited privileges compared to administrative accounts, they still require proper oversight to ensure they are not misused or compromised.
  - Vendor accounts: These accounts are created by the vendor of a technology, such as a firewall or cloud platform. Vendor accounts may or may not have administrative privileges. They are often pre-configured and may come with default credentials that are widely known, creating potential access vulnerabilities if not properly secured or updated.
  - Default accounts: Default accounts are frequently pre-installed on systems or applications and often come with well-known usernames and passwords. These accounts can serve as backdoors if not properly managed or disabled. While some default accounts may be vendor-specific, others may be intended for general use. It is critical to change default credentials immediately and disable any unnecessary default accounts to reduce security risks.
- Implementation of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to protect against unauthorised access. The following requirements outline the use of MFA across various access scenario:
  - Externally / Internet facing applications: All applications that are externally accessible must enforce MFA. This includes applications accessed by employees, contractors, or external users.
  - Remote network access: MFA must be required for all remote access to the NCII entity's network. This applies to virtual private network (VPN) connections, remote desktop protocols (RDP) and any other methods of remote access.
  - Administrative access account: MFA must be enforced for all administrative access accounts, where supported, across all critical assets. This includes systems and applications managed on-site or through third-party service providers.

The IAM procedure shall be reviewed and approved by the relevant cyber security governance committee as authorised by the management for this purpose, ensuring alignment with the NCII entity's organisational goals and adequate allocation of resources for effective implementation.
Approval documentation should be maintained with a revision history to facilitate easy tracking of changes.
Compliance with the IAM procedure, including the provision, revocation and modification of identities shall be regularly monitored to ensure adherence to established policies and controls.

Key compliance monitoring activities may include:
- Conducting periodic audits to verify that identity provisioning, modification and access rights are granted in accordance with the principle of least privilege.
- Monitoring user activity logs to detect and respond to unauthorised access attempts or anomalies.
- Ensuring that access is promptly modified or revoked for employees, contractors, or third parties who no longer require it (due to role changes or termination).
- Utilising automated tools or systems, where feasible, to streamline compliance monitoring, identity lifecycle management, and reporting.

controls.name: Review of Identity and Access Management Procedures

requirements.text: The IAM procedure shall be reviewed at least annually and / or whenever significant changes occur, such as organisational restructuring or updates in access policy.

The review process should include the following activities:
- Evaluate the effectiveness of current IAM controls and identify areas for improvement.
- Update the IAM procedure to address any gaps or changes in the organization's environment.
- Obtain approval from the relevant cyber security governance committee, as authorised by the management approval, for any revisions to the IAM procedure.
- Communicate updates to relevant stakeholders and ensure proper implementation.
The NCII entity shall segregate the duties and responsibilities of employees based on their roles.

Duties and responsibilities shall be segregated to:
- Minimise the risk of unauthorised or fraudulent activities.
- Avoid conflicts of interest by ensuring no individual has complete control over critical processes.
- Prevent blame-shifting by ensuring clear roles definition.

The NCII entity shall clearly define provision roles and responsibilities to ensure:
- Employees are granted appropriate access levels based on their job roles.
- Smooth transitions during role removal or reassignment by predefining and provisioning access requirements.
- Miscommunication and inconsistencies in access provisioning are avoided.
Roles shall be mapped to business processes, and any discrepancies in access provisioning shall be promptly identified and resolved to prevent unauthorised or excessive access.
Segregation of duties shall be reviewed annually, as part of access control audits, and / or whenever there are organisational changes, such as mergers, acquisitions, or role restructurings.

The NCII entity shall establish a PMS by:
- Enforcing password policies, including length, complexity, and expiration periods.
- Ensuring secure storage and retrieval for passwords through encryption.
- Enabling secure password self-service for users to reset their passwords.

The PMS shall adhere to best practices, such as:
- Requiring passwords to include a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols, with at least 8-character password for accounts using MFA and 14-character for accounts not using MFA.
- Prohibiting reuse of the last three passwords.
- Encouraging the use of password managers to securely store and generate strong passwords.

Compliance with password policies shall be monitored by:
- Conducting regular audits of user accounts to ensure adherence to the password policy.
- Generating alerts for weak or compromised passwords using automated tools.
- Reviewing logs from the PMS for anomalies or unauthorised password changes.

The PMS shall be reviewed annually as part of the overall IAM review process, and / or whenever changes occur, such as advancements in password management technologies or the emergence of new cyber security threats like password cracking techniques.

The NCII entity shall establish a Vulnerability Management Procedure (VMP) following the vulnerability management lifecycle as follows:
- Assess: All systems connected to the NCII entity's network must undergo vulnerability scanning.
- Prioritise: Identified vulnerabilities must be prioritised, with critical vulnerabilities addressed first.
- Remediate: A process for remediating identified vulnerabilities must be established, with non-remediable vulnerabilities submitted through the vulnerability exception process.
- Monitor: Security mailing list from the vendors may be subscribed to receive notifications of recently released patches and other software updates, and Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) list data feeds for updates about newly disclosed vulnerabilities.
The procedure shall align with industry standards and best practices such as NIST SP 800-40 Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Planning.

Compliance with the VMP shall be monitored through:
- Regular internal audits to verify adherence to established procedures.
- Reports that measure the effectiveness of vulnerability management activities.

The VMP shall be reviewed and updated annually, to incorporate changes in cyber security threat landscapes or organisational structure, and when changes occur, such as new system implementations or regulatory updates.

The NCII entity shall define a Vulnerability Assessment Plan (VAP) that outline the following:
- The scope of assessments, including networks, applications, systems, and devices.
- The frequency of assessments (monthly, quarterly, or after major updates).
- The tools and methods for identifying vulnerabilities, such as network scanners, penetration tests, and configuration reviews.
- The criteria for categorising vulnerabilities based on criticality and potential impact.
Vulnerability assessments shall be conducted in accordance with the VAP.

The assessments may be carried out through automated vulnerability scanning that utilises tools such as OpenVAS, and manual assessments to identify vulnerabilities that may not be detected by automated tools, including logical flaws and logic vulnerabilities.

The NCII entity shall ensure that vulnerability assessments shall be performed by authorised cyber security professionals, either in-house or through approved third-party experts, to ensure accuracy and reliability.

All identified vulnerabilities shall be managed and remediated through a structured risk-based remediation process, ensuring timely resolution and ongoing security compliance. The NCII entity shall:
- Monitor the vulnerabilities regularly to ensure their status (open, in-progress, resolved) is up to date.
- Continuously monitor vulnerability status (open, in-progress, resolved) to track remediation progress and ensure timely mitigation.
- Integrate threat intelligence feeds to assess vulnerabilities in real time, correlating identified risks with emerging cyber security threats to enhance situational awareness and response effectiveness.

Identified vulnerabilities shall be evaluated using following methods:
- Determine their severity using standards like Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).
- Conduct cyber security risk assessment by evaluating the likelihood of exploitation and the potential consequences on the NCII entity confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA triad).
- Utilise threat intelligence feeds to gain insights into ongoing exploits or emerging attack vectors, enabling prioritisation vulnerabilities that are actively being targeted.
- Assess the value of the affected asset to the NCII entity to determine the level of priority for remediation. Appropriate mitigation and remediation measures shall be implemented based on the evaluation results to address the identified vulnerabilities in a timely manner.

The outcomes of vulnerability assessments and remediation shall be:
- Documented in detailed reports that include:
  - List of vulnerabilities detected.
  - Remediation steps taken.
  - Status updates on unresolved issues.
- Reviewed and presented to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, during periodic review meetings to ensure accountability and drive necessary actions.

The NCII entity shall establish a Security Monitoring Procedure (SMP) that outlines:
- The objectives of security monitoring, including real-time detection of potential cyber security threats, anomalies, and security breaches.
- The scope of monitoring, specifying critical systems, networks, applications, and databases.
- Selection and deployment of monitoring tools such as:
  - IDPS.
  - SIEM solutions.
  - Network and endpoint monitoring tools.
- Defined roles and responsibilities for security operations personnel, including cyber security incident detection, escalation, and response.
- Integration of the monitoring system with threat intelligence feeds to stay updated on evolving cyber security threats.
- Notification and escalation protocols to ensure timely reporting of cyber security incidents to the appropriate stakeholders.

Compliance with the SMP shall be monitored through:
- Regular audits to verify the consistent implementation of monitoring controls and tools.
- Continuous review of monitoring logs to identify gaps or deviations in adherence to the procedure.
- Use of key performance indicators (KPIs) such as:
  - Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) cyber security incidents.
  - Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) to identified cyber security threats.
  - Volume and type of events logged by the monitoring system.
- Assigning accountability to a Cyber Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT) or equivalent group for ensuring compliance.

The SMP shall undergo regular reviews annually, and / or whenever necessary to maintain its effectiveness, through:
- Annual reviews to align the procedure with changes in organisational structure, emerging cyber security threat landscapes and industry standards and regulatory requirements.
- Triggered Reviews whenever significant changes occur, such as:
  - Adoption of new technologies or systems.
  - Major updates to existing infrastructure.

controls.name: Establishment of Cyber Security Incident Management Procedure
controls.parentSection: 18.1 Cyber Security Incident Procedure and Handling

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall establish a Cyber Security Incident Management Procedure (CSIMP) to ensure effective detection, response, and resolution of cyber security incidents. The procedure shall include:
- Cyber Security Incident Response Plan Lifecycle:
  - Plan: Create documentation outlining all procedures required to manage a cyber security incident.
  - Detect: Continuously monitor NCII entity's assets and analyse intelligence to determine if a cyber security incident has occurred.
  - Respond: Implement the cyber security incident response plan to address, manage and recover from the cyber security incident.
  - Update: Evaluate the effectiveness of the cyber security incident response plan, and make necessary updates based on the assessment.
- Roles and Responsibilities:
  - Establish a CSIRT Team or equivalent group which will be responsible for managing the cyber security incident;
  - Assign responsibilities to CSIRT Team or equivalent group;
  - Appointment of three (3) authorised persons responsible for cyber security incident notification as required in subsection 11(3) of Act 854 in the manner prescribed in the Directive of Chief Executive No. 1 - Notification of Cyber Security Incident; and
  - Define roles for stakeholders, including IT, legal, communications, and business units.
- Communication Protocols:
  - Establish internal and external communication channels, including notification to affected stakeholders, management, and regulatory bodies where applicable.

controls.name: Response and Cyber Security Incident Handling
controls.parentSection: 18.1 Cyber Security Incident Procedure and Handling

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall ensure cyber security incidents are responded to and handled in accordance with the CSIMP, compliance to the Act 854 and the Cyber Security (Notification of Cyber Security Incident) Regulations 2024 [P.U. (A) 220/2024] by implementing the following:
- Cyber Security Incident Detection:
  - Use tools like firewalls, intrusion detection systems (IDS), and endpoint protection solutions to detect potential cyber security incidents.
  - The NCII entity's authorised person must immediately notify cyber security incident that has or might have occurred as stated in subregulation 2(1) of P.U. (A) 220/2024 through email at cert@nc4.gov.my.
  - Within six (6) hours from the time the cyber security incident comes to the knowledge of the NCII entity, the authorised person shall submit all necessary information, based on the requirement stated in the subregulation 2(2) of P.U. (A) 220/2024 through NC4's Portal at www.nc4.gov.my.
- Cyber Security Incident Handling Process:
  - Initial Response: Ensure timely triage and categorisation of the cyber security incident.
  - Containment: Take actions to minimise the impact of the cyber security incident, such as isolating affected systems or blocking malicious IP addresses.
  - Eradication: Remove the root cause of the cyber security incident, such as malware or compromised accounts.
  - Recovery: Restore affected systems and ensure normal operations with security enhancements to prevent recurrence.
- Cyber Security Incident Escalation:
  - Escalate cyber security incidents to higher authorities or external specialists based on the severity and complexity of the cyber security incident.
- Documentation and Reporting:
  - Maintain detailed logs of the cyber security incident, including timelines, affected systems, and actions taken.
  - Prepare cyber security incident reports for internal review and compliance purposes.
  - Within fourteen (14) days after the notification, the authorised person shall provide to the fullest extent practicable the supplementary information in the NC4's Portal, as required in subregulation 2(3) of P.U. (A) 220/2024.
  - The authorised person shall provide periodic updates on the reported cyber security incident as required by the Chief Executive, in accordance with subregulation 2(4) of P.U. (A) 220/2024.
  - All report submissions to the Chief Executive of NACSA shall be made through the NC4 system, as stipulated in regulation 3 of P.U. (A) 220/2024. In the event of a disruption, communication shall follow the provisions outlined in paragraph 11 of the Directive of Chief Executive No. 1 - Notification of Cyber Security Incident.
- Engagement with Third Parties:
  - Engage external specialists, such as cyber security incident response consultants, where necessary.
  - Coordinate with law enforcement or regulators for cyber security incidents involving legal violations.

controls.name: Review of Cyber Security Incident Management Procedure
controls.parentSection: 18.1 Cyber Security Incident Procedure and Handling

requirements.text:
The NCII entity shall conduct reviews of the CSIMP annually and / or whenever changes occur to ensure it remains relevant and effective, through:
- Annual Reviews:
  - Reassess the CSIMP annually to account for changes in technology, cyber security threats, and organisational structure.
- Post-Incident Reviews:
  - Conduct a review after significant cyber security incidents to identify strengths, weaknesses, and areas for improvement.
  - Use lessons learned from a cyber security incident or tabletop exercise to enhance the CSIMP.

The CSIMP shall be aligned with regulatory requirements and industry standards, including ISO/IEC 27035: Information Security Incident Management or NIST SP 800-61: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide.
Reviews shall incorporate feedback from IT, legal, and other relevant stakeholders to continuously improve processes.
Version control for CSIMP review documentation shall be maintained to track changes and ensure proper dissemination.

The NCII entity shall establish a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) to ensure critical business functions continue during and after disruptive events. The BCP shall include the following elements:
- Cyber Security Risk Assessment and Business Impact Analysis (BIA):
  - Identify potential cyber security threats (cyber attacks, natural disasters, power failures) and assess their potential impact on business operations.
  - Prioritise critical functions and processes based on their impact on the NCII entity's objectives.
- Crisis Management Framework:
  - Define the structure of the Crisis Management Team (CMT) and their roles and responsibilities.
  - Include clear decision-making protocols and escalation processes.
- Recovery Strategies:
  - Outline strategies to recover critical functions, such as IT system and data recovery, supply chain continuity, and communication plans.
  - Include alternate work arrangements (remote working or alternate office locations).
- Communication Plan:
  - Define internal and external communication procedures during a disruption.
  - Identify communication tools (email, SMS, collaboration platforms) to reach employees, stakeholders, and customers.
- Documentation and Accessibility:
  - Ensure the BCP is documented in detail and made accessible to relevant personnel.
  - Maintain both digital and hard copies stored securely in accessible locations.

The NCII entity shall ensure compliance with the BCP through the following measures:
- Monitoring and Reporting:
  - Regularly monitor adherence to BCP requirements across departments.
  - Generate periodic compliance reports for the review by relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management.
- Audits and Inspections:
  - Conduct scheduled and ad-hoc audits to verify compliance.
  - Ensure findings from audits are addressed and corrective actions are implemented promptly.
- Stakeholder Awareness:
  - Ensure that all employees and stakeholders understand their roles and responsibilities under the BCP.
  - Provide regular updates on BCP processes and requirements to key personnel.
The NCII entity shall review the BCP annually and / or whenever necessary such as after significant events like major cyber security incidents or organisational changes.
During the review process, input from key stakeholders, including employees, suppliers, and customers should be collected.
BCP document should be updated to reflect changes, ensuring proper version control and dissemination to relevant parties.

The NCII entity shall conduct scenario-based testing and simulation exercises on an annual basis or when required by the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management. The exercise shall include all relevant stakeholders to ensure collaboration between the cyber security and business continuity teams.

Realistic scenarios shall be designed that reflect potential disruptions, such as:
- Cyber security incidents (ransomware attacks or data breaches).
- Natural disasters (floods, earthquakes).
- Critical system failures (server outages or power cuts).
- Supply chain disruptions.

The NCII entity should conduct various types of simulations to assess the effectiveness of its BCP and cyber security incident response capabilities, including:
- Tabletop Exercises: Facilitate discussions on how teams would handle specific cyber security incidents and other crises.
- Live Simulations: Conduct full-scale simulations, including cyber attack drills to test response times, coordination, and recovery measures.
- Red Teaming & Penetration Testing: Assess cyber security defences by simulating real-world cyber attacks.

The outcomes of testing and simulations shall be documented and shared with relevant stakeholders through:
- Post-Test Reports:
  - Include findings, cyber resilience performance metrics, and areas needing improvement in post-test reports.
  - Highlight the effectiveness of cyber security incident response strategies and any gaps that were identified.
- Cyber Security Governance Committee Review:
  - Present test results to the relevant cyber security governance committee established for this purpose, as authorised by the management, for review and approval of necessary actions.
  - Align findings with regulatory requirements and sector-wide cyber security strategies.
- Action Plans:
  - Develop and implement corrective action plans to address identified cyber security and business continuity gaps.
  - Assign ownership and deadlines for implementing improvements.
The requirement shall include network segmentation, access control, real-time monitoring, and secure remote access for OT environments.

The NCII entity shall provide a secure e-commerce platform by implementing the following:
- Secure coding practices, regular update and patch CMS platforms, deploy anti-DDoS solutions, and utilize bot detection and prevention measures to protect against web vulnerabilities, fraud, and service disruptions; and
- Compliance with PCI-DSS by using secure payment gateways with encryption and tokenization to ensure online payment security.
